articles.headline: Notice of the Protecting Society of Philadelphia for the Prevention of Kidnapping and Man-stealing
articles.author: John Allen
articles.summary: Notice from the Protecting Society of the city and county of Philadelphia for the preventing of Kidnapping and Man-stealing, auxiliary to the (Pennsylvania) Abolition Society, formed in 1827 to combat the kidnapping of free persons of colour by unprincipled men; persons seeking recovery of kidnapped friends are directed to apply to the Secretary. Signed John Allen, Secretary, Philadelphia, April 24, 1828. (Same standing notice as the May 16 issue.)

articles.headline: Boarding and Lodging house for persons of colour — Gracy Jones (Philadelphia)
articles.author: Gracy Jones
articles.summary: Gracy Jones advertises a boarding and lodging house for genteel persons of colour at No. 8 1/2 South Fourth Street, above Lombard Street, Philadelphia. Dated Philadelphia, April 23, 1828.

articles.headline: Fresh Goshen Butter — David Ruggles (New York)
articles.author: David Ruggles
articles.summary: David Ruggles advertises fresh Goshen butter for sale by the firkin, tub or pound at the cellar under Mr. Whitfield's Stage Office, No. 1 Courtlandt-street, near Broadway, New York. Dated April 8, 1828. (Ruggles later became a prominent Black abolitionist and conductor of the Underground Railroad.)

articles.headline: Land for sale to coloured farmers — Samuel E. Cornish
articles.author: Samuel E. Cornish
articles.summary: Samuel E. Cornish offers 2,000 acres on the banks of the Delaware river (New York state, within 70 miles of the city) to coloured brethren and farmers at $5 an acre and pledges to invest $500 himself. Dated New-York, March 20.

articles.headline: Leghorn and straw hat dressing — Mrs. Sarah Johnson (New York)
articles.author: Sarah Johnson
articles.summary: Mrs. Sarah Johnson, No. 551 Pearl-Street, New York, advertises bleaching, pressing and refitting of Leghorn and straw hats, dressmaking and plain sewing. Dated New-York, April 29, 1828.

articles.headline: House of Refreshment / oysters — William Parker (New York)
articles.author: William Parker
articles.summary: William Parker advertises his House of Refreshment (oysters stewed, fried, or in the shell) at No. 445 Broadway, New York. Dated April 29, 1828.

articles.headline: School for free coloured pupils — Joseph Shippard
articles.author: Joseph Shippard
articles.summary: Joseph Shippard advertises a school for free coloured pupils at his residence on 18th street, teaching English grammar, mercantile arithmetic, geography and mensuration, with board offered for six boys.

articles.headline: African Free Schools notice — Manumission Society of New York
articles.summary: Notice to parents and guardians of coloured children regarding the male and female African Free Schools of the New York Manumission Society (male school in Mulberry-street near Grand-street; female school in William street near Duane street). Signed by order of the Board of Trustees, Peter S. Titus and Richard Field, Jan. 10, 1828.

articles.headline: Boarding house for gentlemen of colour — Richard Johnson (New York)
articles.author: Richard Johnson
articles.summary: Richard Johnson advertises a boarding house for gentlemen of colour at No. 27 Sullivan-Street, New York. Dated New-York Feb. 26, 1828.

articles.headline: Boots, shoes and liquid blacking — W. P. Johnson (New York)
articles.author: W. P. Johnson
articles.summary: W. P. Johnson, 551 Pearl Street near Broadway, New York, sells boots and shoes and liquid blacking of his own manufacture.

articles.headline: G. & R. Draper, coloured tobacco manufacturers (Baltimore)
articles.summary: G. & R. Draper (identified as Coloured Men), Forest-street, Baltimore, manufacture smoking and chewing tobacco, snuff and segars; goods consigned for sale via Samuel E. Cornish in New York.

articles.headline: Prospectus of Freedom's Journal (reprint) and appeal by Russwurm
articles.author: John B. Russwurm
articles.summary: Reprint of the paper's founding Prospectus with an appeal by editor/proprietor John B. Russwurm for continued patronage, noting the Journal has now been published over a year. Dated New-York, April 25, 1828.

articles.headline: Ordination of Rev. Morris Brown as A.M.E. Bishop
articles.summary: Report (Philadelphia, May 26, 1828) that on Sunday May 25th the Rev. Morris Brown of Philadelphia was solemnly ordained, by prayer and the laying on of hands of the Bishop and five ordained Elders, as joint Superintendent or Bishop of the African Methodist Episcopal Church in the United States — described as the second regularly ordained African Bishop in the country. Notes the recent Baltimore Annual Conference and a forthcoming New-York Conference of the A.M.E. Church.

articles.headline: African Methodist Episcopal Church Annual Conference, Philadelphia 1828
articles.summary: Account of the annual conference of the African Methodist Episcopal Church, begun May 5, 1828 in Philadelphia, attended by between thirty and forty preachers from various states including Ohio. On Sunday May 11 Nathan Parman of Chambersburg, Pa. and Israel Scott of New Brunswick were ordained Deacons, and on Sunday May 18 the Rev. Anthony Campbell of Delaware was set apart as an Elder; the meetings are described as a season of revival for the people of Philadelphia.

articles.headline: Convention of the First Coloured Wesleyan Methodist Episcopal Church of New York
articles.summary: Resolutions of a convention of the preachers, trustees, leaders and exhorters of the First Coloured Wesleyan Methodist Episcopal Church of New York, held 14 Feb. 1828, recognizing Rev. Henry Drayton as Superintendent and adopting the Methodist Episcopal discipline. Published by order of the convention; Richard White, President, and Paul Drayton, Secretary. Dated New-York, June 3, 1828.

articles.headline: Reading Room Society of Philadelphia — public address by William Whipper
articles.summary: At a meeting of the Reading Room Society of Philadelphia (for mental improvement), it was resolved that an address be delivered in favour of the Society, and Mr. William Whipper was appointed to deliver it. The committee of arrangements included Messrs. Waterford, Roberts and D. Johnson; the address was set for Thursday the 12th inst. at 8 P.M. in the African Methodist Episcopal church in Lombard street between Fifth and Sixth streets, with tickets from Jacob Gilmore, Joseph Turner (7th below Lombard) and Charles Shorts (Union Hotel, 6th below Pine). Dated Philadelphia, May 2, 1828.

articles.headline: Sermon on Education by Rev. Peter Williams at St. Philip's Church
articles.author: Peter Williams
articles.summary: Original communication printing a sermon on Education delivered in St. Philip's Church, New York, on Sunday 27 April 1828, by the Rev. Peter Williams, on Proverbs 29:15, addressing the responsibility of coloured parents to train up their children. Concluded in the June 13 issue.

articles.headline: Died — Lewis Francis, Clerk of St. Philip's Church (New York)
articles.summary: Died in New York City on Friday evening last, Mr. Lewis Francis, for many years Clerk of St. Philip's Church, the Black Episcopal congregation.

articles.headline: Married at Boston — John C. Stewart to Miss Harry
articles.summary: At Boston on the 25th ult., by the Rev. Samuel Green, Mr. John C. Stewart of Boston married Miss Harry of Worcester, Mass. (The bride's surname is given as Harvey in this issue and corrected to Harry in the June 13 errata.)

articles.headline: The African Schools (New York Manumission Society)
articles.summary: Report (from the Spectator) that the male and female African Free Schools under the Trustees of the New York Manumission Society continue to thrive. Mr. Andrews, long the teacher of the male school, remains in the society's service despite offers elsewhere, and the Rev. Mr. Cornish, an educated coloured clergyman, has for several months served as agent for the Board; a geological and mineralogical cabinet has been established at the Mulberry-street school at Mr. Andrews's suggestion.

articles.headline: Rev. George Erskine to preach in Philadelphia (collection to redeem his children)
articles.summary: Notice that the Rev. George Erskine will preach Sunday morning in the 2d Presbyterian Church of Colour, in Saint Mary's street between 6th and 7th streets, Philadelphia, and in the evening at the Rev. Mr. Livingston's church in Crown-street, with a collection to help redeem his children held in bondage in East Tennessee. Dated Philadelphia, June 11, 1828.

articles.headline: Errata — A.M.E. Conference date and Boston marriage surname
articles.summary: Errata correcting the previous issue: the date of the communication on the Conferences of the A.M.E. Church should read May 5, 1828, and in the Boston marriage notice the bride's surname was Harry, not Harvey as printed.

articles.headline: Fatal scaffold fall in Philadelphia (John Cromwell, bricklayer)
articles.summary: Report that Mr. John Cromwell, a bricklayer, his son, and a black man, while finishing a chimney on a house in Philadelphia, fell when the scaffold gave way; Mr. Cromwell died almost instantly, his son was dangerously hurt, and the coloured man was severely injured. (Race of Cromwell and his son not stated; no person records created for them.)

articles.headline: Melancholy Occurrence — Schuylkill boat drowning, Philadelphia
articles.summary: Report (from the Philadelphia Gazette) that a chartered sail boat carrying nine young men of Philadelphia to the Woodlands on the Schuylkill upset near the U.S. Arsenal and five drowned — Robert Gordon, Alexander Flemming, Charles Rea, Daniel Darrach, and a young lad. John and Samuel McFarland and Henry Smith were among those saved. (Race of the parties not stated; no person records created.)

articles.headline: Boarding and lodging / oysters — Eliza Johnson (New York)
articles.author: Eliza Johnson
articles.summary: Eliza Johnson advertises a boarding and lodging house for genteel persons of colour at No. 24 Elizabeth street, New York, also serving oysters and refreshments.

events.name: Marriage of Amos Dunn (New York)
events.description: Married in New York City on the 25th inst. by the Rev. Mr. Gibbs; both parties of the city (Black marriage column).

events.name: Marriage of Hester Pritchet (New York)
events.description: Married in New York City on Tuesday evening the 27th by the Rev. Mr. Smith; bride Miss Hester Pritchet of the city (groom's surname illegible in the OCR).

events.name: Death of George Davis (New York)
events.description: Died in New York City on the 25th inst., Mr. George Davis, aged 37.

events.name: African Methodist Episcopal Church Annual Conference and ordinations, Philadelphia 1828
events.description: Annual conference of the A.M.E. Church begun May 5, 1828 in Philadelphia with 30-40 preachers from various states. Rev. Morris Brown of Philadelphia was ordained joint Superintendent/Bishop (second ordained African Bishop in the U.S.) on May 25; Nathan Parman of Chambersburg, Pa. and Israel Scott of New Brunswick were ordained Deacons; Rev. Anthony Campbell of Delaware was set apart as an Elder.

events.name: Convention of the First Coloured Wesleyan Methodist Episcopal Church of New York
events.description: Convention of preachers, trustees, leaders and exhorters that recognized Rev. Henry Drayton as Superintendent and adopted the Methodist Episcopal discipline; resolutions published June 3, 1828.

events.name: Reading Room Society of Philadelphia public address
events.location: Philadelphia (A.M.E. church, Lombard street between 5th and 6th)
events.description: Public address delivered on behalf of the Reading Room Society of Philadelphia; William Whipper appointed speaker, with a committee of arrangements and named ticket agents.

events.name: Death of Lewis Francis, Clerk of St. Philip's Church (New York)
events.description: Died in New York City on Friday evening; for many years Clerk of St. Philip's Church, the Black Episcopal congregation.

events.name: Marriage at Boston (Stewart / Harry)
events.description: Married at Boston by the Rev. Samuel Green; groom John C. Stewart of Boston, bride Miss Harry of Worcester (surname corrected from Harvey to Harry in the following issue).

events.name: Rev. George Erskine preaching and collection, Philadelphia
events.description: Rev. George Erskine to preach at the 2d Presbyterian Church of Colour (St. Mary's street between 6th and 7th) in the morning and at the Rev. Mr. Livingston's church in Crown-street in the evening, with a collection to help redeem his children held in bondage in East Tennessee.

agents.note: Freedom's Journal authorised agents roster (masthead list)
Freedom's Journal authorised agents roster (masthead list)
Freedom's Journal authorised agents roster (masthead list)
